speaker: interviewer
text: --number five of our interview with Clara Heller Isaacman. Mrs. Isaacman, all the time that you were in hiding, how did you manage to survive? How did you get food and any other things that you needed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the places we had very little food, very, very little food. And we practically-- there were days we didn't eat. We were lucky we had a piece of bread, a little bit of water, and that was it. But we still remembered that we had some diamonds at my house, where Daddy had shown us what we had. So my mother said she's going to go for them. And we said absolutely not.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then started, who is going for them? Well, my brother said he is. All of us were going, because we knew it was very dangerous to go into that street. And we knew that you can go in but not come out maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: We drew lots. We excluded my mother. She was-- no question about it. We said that we can't live without her. And if one of us will go, so at least she still has two left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we drew lots between the three  of us. And my brother, my youngest brother, got the lot. He was going for the diamonds. And of course, we were very, very nervous and very sick and very afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went one night to Leeuwerikstraat, and it took quite a few hours. And we nearly had given up hope. And there he was at the door, he came back. And for the first time, he laughed, he smiled. I think he could have sang if he wouldn't have been afraid to sing. And he said, I got the lokshn.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't even say, I got the diamonds. What does lokshn mean? Lokshn means noodles. So if you make chicken soup, you put in the noodles. That's the great thing. So he was explaining to mother that he finally got enough diamonds that she can make chicken soup and noodles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from then on, this lokshn remained with us. He got the lokshn. And we were be able to live on that for a while. Then my mother, who had a cigarette box made of 18-karat gold with her two initials with diamonds, had it with her. And she gave it in one of the places, and they sold it. And we lived another six months on that. And that's how we lived from day to day, from week to week, with whatever we had. My mother had a diamond ring with 50 little diamonds that I have now. That she refused to sell. That was my father's engagement ring. And she said she wants one thing to remain with her. And it remained, and I have it. But everything else she had. Now, my diamond that I had went to Blancha. When we were liberated, I took it off my hand and I said, Blancha, here. And she put it on, and then she took it off. And she said, no, it's for you, it's yours. We had quiet discussions. But at the end, she took it, and she's wearing it. I mean, she just died, but she wore it all the time. I like to tell an incident also during the hiding of 1943, Passover, what I think will also show and will remind me and the world what means food, what means strength, what means religion, and most of all, what means a mother. That Passover night, Kroll, I don't know where he got it, but brought us a pound of matzah and a chicken. It wasn't cooked. And we saw this, and we, ha, it was like somebody was bringing in the world to us. We'll finally eat a piece of chicken after a year or so, and we had matzah. We had a pound of matzah. Kroll and my mother-- I don't know if we knew-- but Kroll and my mother knew about a rabbi who was in hiding. And my mother looked at this pound of matzah and the chicken and she says to us, don't get so happy. Don't put your hopes up because this is not for you. We will eat bread if we have. But the rabbi will die if he doesn't have matzah, because he's not going to eat anything else. My mother cooked the chicken, I don't know how. And she asked Kroll to come at night and take me to the rabbi. And I went to the rabbi with the chicken and with the pound of matzah. Now, the rabbi lived in a cubicle. There wasn't even room-- I think he slept on the floor too. But he had a little, not oven, those two burners.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hot plate? Like a hot plate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he said to me, he said, now who's going to light the fire? So I said to Kroll, light the fire for him, it's small. It's Passover. He won't-- he's not putting a match.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Kroll says to me, if he doesn't want to do, let him starve. That was Kroll's-- he felt already too much you want. So I said to Kroll, I said, you know what, you talk to him, and I'll do it. So he talked to him, and I lit the little oven, the flame. And I left the rabbi with a pound of matzah and the chicken.

speaker: interviewee
text: That rabbi was my rabbi for my wedding. And his message to me was, before I went on under the canopy, before I went under the huppah, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he said to me, you're going to America. Remain a Jew. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I think I followed his-- I told him not to worry. Because he knew before he gave me the blessings, they had to send telegrams from America who this Danny Isaacman was. He wanted to make sure that Danny Isaacman was a good Jew, that he had a bar mitzvah, that he was circumcised, and all of this. And I have telegrams that we're going to use that say it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he made sure of this. But he still was afraid that I'm going to go to America and not remain a Jew. And this is already after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry I jumped a little. But I think we had to tell this story. It should be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when this incident happened at Pesach with the rabbi, first of all, what's the rabbi's name? I know the rabbi's name. Halpern. Halpern, not Halpert. That's how I remember it-- E-N, Halpern, OK? I said to him, are you my mother's family? He said no, it's spelled different. All right. And where were you living when that took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: At [NON-ENGLISH] Street. When the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when that incident took place.

speaker: interviewee
text: At Stadion Street we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a little later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the last Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Between Mrs. Brussels and Stadion Street, did you make any other stops?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When we were at Mrs. Brussels, Kroll, went to Stadion Street, and he found an empty house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a big house, a row house. There was practically no furniture in it. At the back of the house was a wall, a tall wall. I think that what made him decide that he can hide there a lot of Jews. And when Mrs. Brussels decided that she was afraid that somebody again will tell on her, she told Kroll and Blancha-- Blancha was part of it too, And von Oste was part of it too, her husband. And they all decided that it would be OK if we go to Stadion street and live there. Now, Stadion Street was outside of Antwerp, it's called Hoboken. And we went there. They took us at night, and we went there. And there they filled the house with a lot of strange people, Jews, who had wandered from one place to the other, and just like us were still alive. One of them had, I'm sure, lost his mind because he was there, but he didn't know what he was doing. Used to open the door, used to open a window, what he wasn't allowed to. And one day, he walked out and never came back. They must have caught him, the Gestapo. Some of them just left by themselves. There were a lot of times that they brought in a child, young kids, 10, 12, 15. And the other people didn't want them. My mother always said, if my child is here, this one has to be here, until they find a place for it. And I remember quite a few arguments. I remember once I slept on the steps of the house, because my mother wanted this child to sleep with her, near her, like I used to sleep near her. Because she was afraid that one of the men or one of the women, somebody there, will get that child out of the house, because that child used to cry a lot. It was Anika. And Anika was with us in another place too. But Anika now is in Antwerp living and has a row of business in one street.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's Anika's last name, or what was her last name--

speaker: interviewee
text: Noble.

speaker: interviewer
text: --During the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said I won't need this. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did Anika come to be  in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anika-- one person from the Underground, Anika had been with her brother and her father hidden someplace. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] somebody had told that Jews are there, what always happened. That's why we had to run from one place to the other. And they had to divide the three of them. They couldn't find a place for all of them together. And they brought Anika to us. Excuse me. Now, Anika one night decided she's going to visit her father. She knew where her father was. And she made herself a mark, that the door was green or yellow, and that she will recognize as soon as she gets in that street. There's no door that color. That's the only door that has this color. And when she came back, she couldn't find the color of the door. And she walked in the streets and she walked in the streets. And she knew that if it comes in the morning, she will be caught by the Gestapo. All at once, she realized that the door had that color inside, not outside. And she tried to recollect which house she was in, and she found the house. But up till today, she tells this story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she reunited with her father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was, after that, reunited with her father and with her brother too. I see him a lot when I go there. Her father's dead already, but Anika I see and her brother too.

speaker: interviewee
text: How long were you  at Stadionstrasse? Until the liberation, quite a few months. I remember D-Day. I remember Normandy. By the way, Normandy, my husband landed in Normandy. He was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And von Oste came to us one night, not late. I remember it so well. And he said, we're going to be liberated. But don't go outside until I send somebody, or I come to tell you that you're safe enough. Now, you will hear all kinds of things. You will hear cheers. You will maybe even see prisoners of war in the street. Don't go out until I come to tell you. And it took two days. And we were waiting. And we heard in the streets all kinds of things. But my mother, of course, didn't let us even look out. Then von Oste himself came and said, I'm going to leave. I don't want to be seen-- he was in the uniform. But in an hour or two, you can walk out. You're free. And my mother and all of us-- my sister said, can I sing my first aria. So my mother said, if you want to, go ahead. And my brother said, I want a gun. I want to shoot every German I'll ever see. And my mother said, no, we don't kill. I can understand how you feel about it. But we don't kill.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I said anything. I just was wondering if anybody else survived, if any of my friends survived or any-- my neighbors or any Jew. Is there still another Jew in Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked out, and we opened the door. And the next-door neighbor apparently thought that there are Germans living in that house. And he was with the Belgian Underground. And he came over with his gun. And he was ready to shoot. And he said, I suffered enough knowing I have Germans living near me. And I don't know what I'm going to do, whatever. And across the street, there was another man who was living-- who was from the Underground, a policeman. And he ran over, and he said, those are Jews. You just didn't know. I knew that there were Jews living here. And I fainted. And my mother, of course, I guess-- I didn't see it-- got panicky and so on. But I fainted because of the air. I wasn't used to  anymore fresh air. And I came back, and we were liberated. And two hours later, we really saw hundreds of prisoners of war who were taken to camps, to get putting together. And the first soldiers that we saw weren't the Americans. They were the English soldiers. The Americans came in two days later, two or three days later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in Antwerp at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: In general, life was OK. When I was liberated, the first thing I wanted to do is-- we were in Hoboken. I wanted to go back to my home, to my street, to the streets that I was used to. And my mother didn't let us. Finally, she agreed after a few days. So my sister and I started walking in the streets there. And whoever passed us, we wanted to say, hey, you know, I'm alive. You know who I am and so on. But of course, just like here, I wouldn't ask anybody, why are you passing me or so. And then we started looking for Jews. But we couldn't find in the beginning. Then once in a while, second day or the third day, we walked the streets and somebody passed us, and it was like [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] magnets. We felt somebody pulling us like.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we turned around and we said in Yiddish, are you a Jew? And they said yes. And that's how we started finding a few Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, I don't know, for the first time, it was a week later or two weeks, I decided finally to go to my home, to my street, to Leeuwerikstraat. I knew where Leeuwerikstraat was. But I walked around Leeuwerikstraat maybe for two hours without getting into Leeuwerikstraat. I couldn't find it. I could find it. I guess I just didn't want to walk in there. And finally, when I walked in there, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] the rocks were crying under my feet, because there was nobody there. I went over to my door. I knocked at the door. Nobody answered. It was empty, but there was nothing there. Everything was ransacked, everything was taken. I took  later on my husband to see it. The liberation did a lot to us. I am a hidden child. I think we should all remember that when we say survivors, that there are different kinds of survivors. And each one of us feel our story is the most important, should be told, what everybody feels. But nobody can feel what the other one went through. The survivors from concentration camp, after I saw concentration camps-- and I didn't see it until '82-- I don't know how any of them came out, not one of them. And I feel that each one of them has a hidden story, just like I do. I'm not telling my story. I'm telling my story, but I have a hidden story. The children of the survivors, the hidden children, have quite a story too. Then there is the Resistance survivors, who were hidden in the woods. And they have a different story. But all of our stories are stories that if the human race doesn't learn from them, they won't learn from anything else. Because there is so much to think about and to wonder.

speaker: interviewee
text: I myself wonder. How could I have done this for two and a half years? Even if I tell it in six hours, in 10 hours, in a month, this was two and a half years. This was two and a half years of my life that was taken without any reason, only because I was born Jewish. And I think that's part of us survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we're telling our story, sometimes we tell it as a matter of fact. It's not as a matter of fact. We tell it as it is, but it goes fast. Our minutes and hours and days went very, very slow and scary. I have a fifth sense, a sixth sense. I can hear a bird singing outside, in the middle of the night, because I listened. My whole two and a half years was listening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are they coming for me? Am I going to live tomorrow? Are they going to kill me tomorrow? What's tomorrow going to bring? Am I going to be able to breathe again free?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I guess, here I am, breathing free, but not as free as I would like to. Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we know. When I came here to the United States of America in 1946, the United States of America was less anti-Semitic, less prejudiced, less hatred between one and the other. It was less of a lot of things that we should try and get it back like it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to right after the war. How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] had left the University of Pennsylvania and volunteered to fight the war. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Because his father had a drugstore, he didn't carry a gun. He was put right away in the medics. And he landed in Normandy. He delivered a baby in Normandy, landing in Normandy, with his American Army. It's quite a story, but not now. And then he came to Antwerp, to the Scheldt, where he was there with the army unit and the medics unit. He gave over 100 injections a day. He learned how to give them, I guess, there. And he was, in America, educated as a very big patriot but as a Jew, a very dedicated Jew, not an Orthodox Jew, far from it. And when he got to Europe, he decided he wants to see and he wants to hear what happened to the Jews. And he-- in Antwerp, started looking for survivors. Somebody told him that there were a group of young boys and girls, 10 to 17 and so, who are meeting once a week in a little room. And he decided to come to that little room and meet those young people who survived.
